## DEFORMABLE DETR: DEFORMABLE TRANSFORMERS - FOR END TO-END OBJECT DETECTION
**Xizhou Zhu**[1] _[∗]_ **, Weijie Su**[2] _[∗‡]_ **, Lewei Lu**[1] **, Bin Li**[2] **, Xiaogang Wang**[1] _[,]_[3] **, Jifeng Dai**[1] _[†]_

1SenseTime Research

2University of Science and Technology of China

3The Chinese University of Hong Kong
_{_ zhuwalter,luotto,daijifeng _}_ @sensetime.com jackroos@mail.ustc.edu.cn, binli@ustc.edu.cn xgwang@ee.cuhk.edu.hk

## ABSTRACT

DETR has been recently proposed to eliminate the need for many hand-designed components in object detection while demonstrating good performance. However, it suffers from slow convergence and limited feature spatial resolution, due to the limitation of Transformer attention modules in processing image feature maps. To mitigate these issues, we proposed Deformable DETR, whose attention modules only attend to a small set of key sampling points around a reference. Deformable DETR can achieve better performance than DETR (especially on small objects) with 10 _×_ less training epochs. Extensive experiments on the COCO benchmark demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach. Code is released at https:// github.com/fundamentalvision/Deformable-DETR.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Modern object detectors employ many hand-crafted components (Liu et al., 2020), e.g., anchor generation, rule-based training target assignment, non-maximum suppression (NMS) post-processing. They are not fully end-to-end. Recently, Carion et al. (2020) proposed DETR to eliminate the need for such hand-crafted components, and built the first fully end-to-end object detector, achieving very competitive performance. DETR utilizes a simple architecture, by combining convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) encoder-decoders. They exploit the versatile and powerful relation modeling capability of Transformers to replace the hand-crafted rules, under properly designed training signals.

Despite its interesting design and good performance, DETR has its own issues: (1) It requires much longer training epochs to converge than the existing object detectors. For example, on the COCO (Lin et al., 2014) benchmark, DETR needs 500 epochs to converge, which is around 10 to 20 times slower than Faster R-CNN (Ren et al., 2015). (2) DETR delivers relatively low performance at detecting small objects. Modern object detectors usually exploit multi-scale features, where small objects are detected from high-resolution feature maps. Meanwhile, high-resolution feature maps lead to unacceptable complexities for DETR. The above-mentioned issues can be mainly attributed to the deficit of Transformer components in processing image feature maps. At initialization, the attention modules cast nearly uniform attention weights to all the pixels in the feature maps. Long training epoches is necessary for the attention weights to be learned to focus on sparse meaningful locations. On the other hand, the attention weights computation in Transformer encoder is of quadratic computation w.r.t. pixel numbers. Thus, it is of very high computational and memory complexities to process high-resolution feature maps.

In the image domain, deformable convolution (Dai et al., 2017) is of a powerful and efficient mechanism to attend to sparse spatial locations. It naturally avoids the above-mentioned issues. While it lacks the element relation modeling mechanism, which is the key for the success of DETR.

In this paper, we propose _Deformable DETR_ , which mitigates the slow convergence and high complexity issues of DETR. It combines the best of the sparse spatial sampling of deformable convolution, and the relation modeling capability of Transformers. We propose the _deformable attention module_ , which attends to a small set of sampling locations as a pre-filter for prominent key elements out of all the feature map pixels. The module can be naturally extended to aggregating multi-scale features, without the help of FPN (Lin et al., 2017a). In Deformable DETR , we utilize (multi-scale) deformable attention modules to replace the Transformer attention modules processing feature maps, as shown in Fig. 1.

Deformable DETR opens up possibilities for us to exploit variants of end-to-end object detectors, thanks to its fast convergence, and computational and memory efficiency. We explore a simple and effective _iterative bounding box refinement_ mechanism to improve the detection performance. We also try a _two-stage Deformable DETR_ , where the region proposals are also generated by a vaiant of Deformable DETR, which are further fed into the decoder for iterative bounding box refinement.

Extensive experiments on the COCO (Lin et al., 2014) benchmark demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach. Compared with DETR, Deformable DETR can achieve better performance (especially on small objects) with 10 _×_ less training epochs. The proposed variant of two-stage Deformable DETR can further improve the performance. Code is released at https://github. com/fundamentalvision/Deformable-DETR.

## 2 RELATED WORK

**Efficient Attention Mechanism.** Transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017) involve both self-attention and cross-attention mechanisms. One of the most well-known concern of Transformers is the high time and memory complexity at vast key element numbers, which hinders model scalability in many cases. Recently, many efforts have been made to address this problem (Tay et al., 2020b), which can be roughly divided into three categories in practice.

The first category is to use pre-defined sparse attention patterns on keys. The most straightforward paradigm is restricting the attention pattern to be fixed local windows. Most works (Liu et al., 2018a; Parmar et al., 2018; Child et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2019; Ho et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020a; Hu et al., 2019; Ramachandran et al., 2019; Qiu et al., 2019; Beltagy et al., 2020; Ainslie et al., 2020; Zaheer et al., 2020) follow this paradigm. Although restricting the attention pattern to a local neighborhood can decrease the complexity, it loses global information. To compensate, Child et al. (2019); Huang et al. (2019); Ho et al. (2019); Wang et al. (2020a) attend key elements

2

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

at fixed intervals to significantly increase the receptive field on keys. Beltagy et al. (2020); Ainslie et al. (2020); Zaheer et al. (2020) allow a small number of special tokens having access to all key elements. Zaheer et al. (2020); Qiu et al. (2019) also add some pre-fixed sparse attention patterns to attend distant key elements directly.

The second category is to learn data-dependent sparse attention. Kitaev et al. (2020) proposes a locality sensitive hashing (LSH) based attention, which hashes both the query and key elements to different bins. A similar idea is proposed by Roy et al. (2020), where k-means finds out the most related keys. Tay et al. (2020a) learns block permutation for block-wise sparse attention.

The third category is to explore the low-rank property in self-attention. Wang et al. (2020b) reduces the number of key elements through a linear projection on the size dimension instead of the channel dimension. Katharopoulos et al. (2020); Choromanski et al. (2020) rewrite the calculation of selfattention through kernelization approximation.

In the image domain, the designs of efficient attention mechanism (e.g., Parmar et al. (2018); Child et al. (2019); Huang et al. (2019); Ho et al. (2019); Wang et al. (2020a); Hu et al. (2019); Ramachandran et al. (2019)) are still limited to the first category. Despite the theoretically reduced complexity, Ramachandran et al. (2019); Hu et al. (2019) admit such approaches are much slower in implementation than traditional convolution with the same FLOPs (at least 3 _×_ slower), due to the intrinsic limitation in memory access patterns.

On the other hand, as discussed in Zhu et al. (2019a), there are variants of convolution, such as deformable convolution (Dai et al., 2017; Zhu et al., 2019b) and dynamic convolution (Wu et al., 2019), that also can be viewed as self-attention mechanisms. Especially, deformable convolution operates much more effectively and efficiently on image recognition than Transformer self-attention. Meanwhile, it lacks the element relation modeling mechanism.

Our proposed deformable attention module is inspired by deformable convolution, and belongs to the second category. It only focuses on a small fixed set of sampling points predicted from the feature of query elements. Different from Ramachandran et al. (2019); Hu et al. (2019), deformable attention is just slightly slower than the traditional convolution under the same FLOPs.

**Multi-scale Feature Representation for Object Detection.** One of the main difficulties in object detection is to effectively represent objects at vastly different scales. Modern object detectors usually exploit multi-scale features to accommodate this. As one of the pioneering works, FPN (Lin et al., 2017a) proposes a top-down path to combine multi-scale features. PANet (Liu et al., 2018b) further adds an bottom-up path on the top of FPN. Kong et al. (2018) combines features from all scales by a global attention operation. Zhao et al. (2019) proposes a U-shape module to fuse multi-scale features. Recently, NAS-FPN (Ghiasi et al., 2019) and Auto-FPN (Xu et al., 2019) are proposed to automatically design cross-scale connections via neural architecture search. Tan et al. (2020) proposes the BiFPN, which is a repeated simplified version of PANet. Our proposed multi-scale deformable attention module can naturally aggregate multi-scale feature maps via attention mechanism, without the help of these feature pyramid networks.

## 3 REVISITING TRANSFORMERS AND DETR

**Multi-Head Attention in Transformers.** Transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017) are of a network architecture based on attention mechanisms for machine translation. Given a query element (e.g., a target word in the output sentence) and a set of key elements (e.g., source words in the input sentence), the _multi-head attention module_ adaptively aggregates the key contents according to the attention weights that measure the compatibility of query-key pairs. To allow the model focusing on contents from different representation subspaces and different positions, the outputs of different attention heads are linearly aggregated with learnable weights. There are two known issues with Transformers. One is Transformers need long training schedules before convergence. Suppose the number of query and key elements are of _Nq_ and _Nk_ , respectively. Typically, with proper parameter initialization, _**U** m_ _**z** q_ and _**V** m_ _**x** k_ follow distribution with mean of 1 0 and variance of 1, which makes attention weights _Amqk ≈ Nk_[,][when] _[N][k]_[is][large.][It][will][lead] to ambiguous gradients for input features. Thus, long training schedules are required so that the attention weights can focus on specific keys. In the image domain, where the key elements are usually of image pixels, _Nk_ can be very large and the convergence is tedious.

On the other hand, the computational and memory complexity for multi-head attention can be very high with numerous query and key elements. The computational complexity of Eq. 1 is of _O_ ( _NqC_[2] + _NkC_[2] + _NqNkC_ ). In the image domain, where the query and key elements are both of pixels, _Nq_ = _Nk ≫ C_ , the complexity is dominated by the third term, as _O_ ( _NqNkC_ ). Thus, the multi-head attention module suffers from a quadratic complexity growth with the feature map size.

**DETR.** DETR (Carion et al., 2020) is built upon the Transformer encoder-decoder architecture, combined with a set-based Hungarian loss that forces unique predictions for each ground-truth bounding box via bipartite matching. We briefly review the network architecture as follows.

Given the input feature maps _**x** ∈_ R _[C][×][H][×][W]_ extracted by a CNN backbone (e.g., ResNet (He et al., 2016)), DETR exploits a standard Transformer encoder-decoder architecture to transform the input feature maps to be features of a set of object queries. A 3-layer feed-forward neural network (FFN) and a linear projection are added on top of the object query features (produced by the decoder) as the detection head. The FFN acts as the regression branch to predict the bounding box coordinates _**b** ∈_ [0 _,_ 1][4] , where _**b**_ = _{bx, by, bw, bh}_ encodes the normalized box center coordinates, box height and width (relative to the image size). The linear projection acts as the classification branch to produce the classification results.

For the Transformer encoder in DETR, both query and key elements are of pixels in the feature maps. The inputs are of ResNet feature maps (with encoded positional embeddings). Let _H_ and _W_ denote the feature map height and width, respectively. The computational complexity of self-attention is of _O_ ( _H_[2] _W_[2] _C_ ), which grows quadratically with the spatial size.

For the Transformer decoder in DETR, the input includes both feature maps from the encoder, and _N_ object queries represented by learnable positional embeddings (e.g., _N_ = 100). There are two types of attention modules in the decoder, namely, cross-attention and self-attention modules. In the cross-attention modules, object queries extract features from the feature maps. The query elements are of the object queries, and key elements are of the output feature maps from the encoder. In it, _Nq_ = _N_ , _Nk_ = _H × W_ and the complexity of the cross-attention is of _O_ ( _HWC_[2] + _NHWC_ ). The complexity grows linearly with the spatial size of feature maps. In the self-attention modules, object queries interact with each other, so as to capture their relations. The query and key elements are both of the object queries. In it, _Nq_ = _Nk_ = _N_ , and the complexity of the self-attention module is of _O_ (2 _NC_[2] + _N_[2] _C_ ). The complexity is acceptable with moderate number of object queries.

DETR is an attractive design for object detection, which removes the need for many hand-designed components. However, it also has its own issues. These issues can be mainly attributed to the deficits of Transformer attention in handling image feature maps as key elements: (1) DETR has relatively low performance in detecting small objects. Modern object detectors use high-resolution feature maps to better detect small objects. However, high-resolution feature maps would lead to an unacceptable complexity for the self-attention module in the Transformer encoder of DETR, which has a quadratic complexity with the spatial size of input feature maps. (2) Compared with modern object detectors, DETR requires many more training epochs to converge. This is mainly because the attention modules processing image features are difficult to train. For example, at initialization, the cross-attention modules are almost of average attention on the whole feature maps. While, at the end of the training, the attention maps are learned to be very sparse, focusing only on the object

## 4 METHOD

## 4.1 DEFORMABLE TRANSFORMERS FOR END-TO-END OBJECT DETECTION

**Deformable Attention Module.** The core issue of applying Transformer attention on image feature maps is that it would look over all possible spatial locations. To address this, we present a _deformable attention module_ . Inspired by deformable convolution (Dai et al., 2017; Zhu et al., 2019b), the deformable attention module only attends to a small set of key sampling points around a reference point, regardless of the spatial size of the feature maps, as shown in Fig. 2. By assigning only a small fixed number of keys for each query, the issues of convergence and feature spatial resolution can be mitigated.

**Deformable Transformer Encoder.** We replace the Transformer attention modules processing feature maps in DETR with the proposed multi-scale deformable attention module. Both the input and output of the encoder are of multi-scale feature maps with the same resolutions. In encoder, we extract multi-scale feature maps _{_ _**x**[l] }[L] l_ =1 _[−]_[1][(] _[L]_[ = 4][) from the output feature maps of stages] _[ C]_[3][ through] _C_ 5 in ResNet (He et al., 2016) (transformed by a 1 _×_ 1 convolution), where _Cl_ is of resolution 2 _[l]_ lower than the input image. The lowest resolution feature map _**x**[L]_ is obtained via a 3 _×_ 3 stride 2 convolution on the final _C_ 5 stage, denoted as _C_ 6. All the multi-scale feature maps are of _C_ = 256 channels. Note that the top-down structure in FPN (Lin et al., 2017a) is not used, because our proposed multi-scale deformable attention in itself can exchange information among multi-scale feature maps. The constructing of multi-scale feature maps are also illustrated in Appendix A.2. Experiments in Section 5.2 show that adding FPN will not improve the performance. In application of the multi-scale deformable attention module in encoder, the output are of multiscale feature maps with the same resolutions as the input. Both the key and query elements are of pixels from the multi-scale feature maps. For each query pixel, the reference point is itself. To identify which feature level each query pixel lies in, we add a scale-level embedding, denoted as _**e** l_ , to the feature representation, in addition to the positional embedding. Different from the positional embedding with fixed encodings, the scale-level embedding _{_ _**e** l}[L] l_ =1[are][randomly][initialized][and] jointly trained with the network.

**Deformable Transformer Decoder.** There are cross-attention and self-attention modules in the decoder. The query elements for both types of attention modules are of object queries. In the crossattention modules, object queries extract features from the feature maps, where the key elements are of the output feature maps from the encoder. In the self-attention modules, object queries interact with each other, where the key elements are of the object queries. Since our proposed deformable attention module is designed for processing convolutional feature maps as key elements, we only replace each cross-attention module to be the multi-scale deformable attention module, while leaving the self-attention modules unchanged. For each object query, the 2-d normalized coordinate of the

reference point _**p**_ ˆ _q_ is predicted from its object query embedding via a learnable linear projection followed by a sigmoid function.

Because the multi-scale deformable attention module extracts image features around the reference point, we let the detection head predict the bounding box as relative offsets w.r.t. the reference point to further reduce the optimization difficulty. The reference point is used as the initial guess of the box center. The detection head predicts the relative offsets w.r.t. the reference point. Check Appendix A.3 for the details. In this way, the learned decoder attention will have strong correlation with the predicted bounding boxes, which also accelerates the training convergence.
By replacing Transformer attention modules with deformable attention modules in DETR, we establish an efficient and fast converging detection system, dubbed as Deformable DETR (see Fig. 1).

## 4.2 ADDITIONAL IMPROVEMENTS AND VARIANTS FOR DEFORMABLE DETR

Deformable DETR opens up possibilities for us to exploit various variants of end-to-end object detectors, thanks to its fast convergence, and computational and memory efficiency. Due to limited space, we only introduce the core ideas of these improvements and variants here. The implementation details are given in Appendix A.4.

**Iterative Bounding Box Refinement.** This is inspired by the iterative refinement developed in optical flow estimation (Teed & Deng, 2020). We establish a simple and effective iterative bounding box refinement mechanism to improve detection performance. Here, each decoder layer refines the bounding boxes based on the predictions from the previous layer.

**Two-Stage Deformable DETR.** In the original DETR, object queries in the decoder are irrelevant to the current image. Inspired by two-stage object detectors, we explore a variant of Deformable DETR for generating region proposals as the first stage. The generated region proposals will be fed into the decoder as object queries for further refinement, forming a two-stage Deformable DETR.

In the first stage, to achieve high-recall proposals, each pixel in the multi-scale feature maps would serve as an object query. However, directly setting object queries as pixels will bring unacceptable computational and memory cost for the self-attention modules in the decoder, whose complexity grows quadratically with the number of queries. To avoid this problem, we remove the decoder and form an encoder-only Deformable DETR for region proposal generation. In it, each pixel is assigned as an object query, which directly predicts a bounding box. Top scoring bounding boxes are picked as region proposals. No NMS is applied before feeding the region proposals to the second stage.

## 5 EXPERIMENT

**Dataset.** We conduct experiments on COCO 2017 dataset (Lin et al., 2014). Our models are trained on the train set, and evaluated on the val set and test-dev set.

**Implementation Details.** ImageNet (Deng et al., 2009) pre-trained ResNet-50 (He et al., 2016) is utilized as the backbone for ablations. Multi-scale feature maps are extracted without FPN (Lin et al., 2017a). _M_ = 8 and _K_ = 4 are set for deformable attentions by default. Parameters of the deformable Transformer encoder are shared among different feature levels. Other hyper-parameter setting and training strategy mainly follow DETR (Carion et al., 2020), except that Focal Loss (Lin et al., 2017b) with loss weight of 2 is used for bounding box classification, and the number of object queries is increased from 100 to 300. We also report the performance of DETR-DC5 with these modifications for a fair comparison, denoted as DETR-DC5[+] . By default, models are trained for 50 epochs and the learning rate is decayed at the 40-th epoch by a factor of 0.1. Following DETR(Carion et al., 2020), we train our models using Adam optimizer (Kingma & Ba, 2015) with base learning rate of 2 _×_ 10 _[−]_[4] , _β_ 1 = 0 _._ 9, _β_ 2 = 0 _._ 999, and weight decay of 10 _[−]_[4] . Learning rates of the linear projections, used for predicting object query reference points and sampling offsets, are multiplied by a factor of 0.1. Run time is evaluated on NVIDIA Tesla V100 GPU.

## 5.1 COMPARISON WITH DETR

As shown in Table 1, compared with Faster R-CNN + FPN, DETR requires many more training epochs to converge, and delivers lower performance at detecting small objects. Compared with

7

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

DETR, Deformable DETR achieves better performance (especially on small objects) with 10 _×_ less training epochs. Detailed convergence curves are shown in Fig. 3. With the aid of iterative bounding box refinement and two-stage paradigm, our method can further improve the detection accuracy.

Our proposed Deformable DETR has on par FLOPs with Faster R-CNN + FPN and DETR-DC5. But the runtime speed is much faster (1.6 _×_ ) than DETR-DC5, and is just 25% slower than Faster R-CNN + FPN. The speed issue of DETR-DC5 is mainly due to the large amount of memory access in Transformer attention. Our proposed deformable attention can mitigate this issue, at the cost of unordered memory access. Thus, it is still slightly slower than traditional convolution.

## 5.2 ABLATION STUDY ON DEFORMABLE ATTENTION

Table 2 presents ablations for various design choices of the proposed deformable attention module. Using multi-scale inputs instead of single-scale inputs can effectively improve detection accuracy with 1 _._ 7% AP, especially on small objects with 2 _._ 9% APS. Increasing the number of sampling points _K_ can further improve 0 _._ 9% AP. Using multi-scale deformable attention, which allows information exchange among different scale levels, can bring additional 1 _._ 5% improvement in AP. Because the cross-level feature exchange is already adopted, adding FPNs will not improve the performance. When multi-scale attention is not applied, and _K_ = 1, our (multi-scale) deformable attention module degenerates to deformable convolution, delivering noticeable lower accuracy.

## 5.3 COMPARISON WITH STATE-OF-THE-ART METHODS

Table 3 compares the proposed method with other state-of-the-art methods. Iterative bounding box refinement and two-stage mechanism are both utilized by our models in Table 3. With ResNet-101 and ResNeXt-101 (Xie et al., 2017), our method achieves 48.7 AP and 49.0 AP without bells and whistles, respectively. By using ResNeXt-101 with DCN (Zhu et al., 2019b), the accuracy rises to 50.1 AP. With additional test-time augmentations, the proposed method achieves 52.3 AP.

## 6 CONCLUSION

Deformable DETR is an end-to-end object detector, which is efficient and fast-converging. It enables us to explore more interesting and practical variants of end-to-end object detectors. At the core of Deformable DETR are the (multi-scale) deformable attention modules, which is an efficient attention mechanism in processing image feature maps. We hope our work opens up new possibilities in exploring end-to-end object detection.

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper is high-quality and addresses an important limitation of DETR: slow convergence and poor small-object performance caused by dense global attention over high-resolution image features. The motivation is compelling, the proposed idea of replacing dense feature-map attention with sparse deformable attention is well aligned with the diagnosis, and the introduction clearly positions the work as combining the efficiency of deformable convolution with the relational modeling of Transformers. The contribution also appears practically significant, especially the reported 10× reduction in training epochs and improved multi-scale detection without relying on a standard FPN. That said, the early presentation has some clarity and polish issues: there are several grammatical errors and typos (“epoches,” “vaiant,” awkward phrasing such as “is of a powerful”), and the paper could more explicitly distinguish deformable attention from prior sparse/data-dependent attention mechanisms at the conceptual level before moving into related work. The most significant issue to scrutinize is whether the proposed method preserves DETR’s end-to-end simplicity or reintroduces task-specific inductive biases comparable to those it aims to avoid; the paper should clearly justify this trade-off and provide ablations showing which components—multi-scale deformable attention, iterative refinement, and two-stage proposal generation—drive the gains. Overall, the work appears strong, impactful, and well motivated, but its claims would benefit from sharper exposition, cleaner writing, and careful empirical separation of the contributions.

comments.title: Overbroad claim that DETR was the first fully end-to-end object detector
comments.quote: Recently, Carion et al. (2020) proposed DETR to eliminate the need for such hand-crafted components, and built the first fully end-to-end object detector, achieving very competitive performance.
comments.explanation: The intended contrast is understandable: DETR removes anchors, heuristic many-to-one target assignment, and NMS, and instead uses a set-prediction formulation. However, the unqualified phrase “the first fully end-to-end object detector” is too broad. Earlier detectors were often trained end-to-end in the usual deep-learning sense, even if they retained proposals, anchors, or post-processing. DETR also still uses a hand-designed Hungarian matching cost during training. A more precise claim would be that DETR was among the first competitive detectors to formulate detection as direct set prediction without anchors and NMS.

comments.title: Attention-initialization explanation for DETR’s slow convergence is overstated
comments.quote: The above-mentioned issues can be mainly attributed to the deficit of Transformer components in processing image feature maps. At initialization, the attention modules cast nearly uniform attention weights to all the pixels in the feature maps. Long training epoches is necessary for the attention weights to be learned to focus on sparse meaningful locations.
comments.explanation: The intuition is plausible: standard dense attention over image features is expensive, and randomly initialized attention may initially lack spatial selectivity. However, the paper states the causal explanation too strongly. DETR’s slow convergence may also be affected by Hungarian matching, randomly initialized object queries, weak dense spatial priors, loss design, and optimization details. In the later formal discussion, the claim that initialized attention weights are approximately uniform also follows only in expectation by symmetry; individual softmax weights need not be close to uniform unless additional assumptions on logit variance and concentration are made. The argument would be safer if framed as a motivating hypothesis or one contributing factor rather than the main demonstrated cause of long training schedules.

comments.title: Epoch-count comparison could be misread as wall-clock training speed
comments.quote: For example, on the COCO (Lin et al., 2014) benchmark, DETR needs 500 epochs to converge, which is around 10 to 20 times slower than Faster R-CNN (Ren et al., 2015).
comments.explanation: The qualitative point is valid: DETR requires far more training epochs than common Faster R-CNN COCO schedules. However, “10 to 20 times slower” can imply wall-clock time or computational efficiency, while the evidence stated is only epoch count. Epoch cost differs between DETR and Faster R-CNN, and Faster R-CNN schedules vary across implementations. The comparison would be more precise as “requires roughly 10–20× more training epochs,” with the Faster R-CNN schedule specified.

comments.title: Decoder cross-attention complexity omits the query-projection term
comments.quote: In it, Nq = N, Nk = H × W and the complexity of the cross-attention is of O(HWC² + NHWC).
comments.explanation: The paper first gives standard multi-head attention complexity as O(NqC² + NkC² + NqNkC). Substituting Nq = N and Nk = H × W gives O(NC² + HWC² + NHWC), under the same accounting convention. The quoted expression drops the NC² query-projection term. This omission may be intentional because N is usually much smaller than HW and the authors are emphasizing feature-map scaling, but as written the expression is technically incomplete relative to the formula just stated.

comments.title: Full sampling does not make deformable attention equivalent to standard Transformer attention
comments.quote: When the sampling points traverse all possible locations, the proposed attention module is equivalent to Transformer attention.
comments.explanation: The intended analogy is that deformable attention is a sparse version of attention over image locations. However, even if the sampled locations covered every pixel, the module would not recover standard Transformer attention as defined earlier. In deformable attention, sampling offsets and attention weights are produced from the query feature; the weights are not computed from pairwise query-key content similarities. Full sampling would therefore give dense learned spatial aggregation, but not ordinary content-based Transformer attention. The claim should be weakened to say that the module would have dense spatial support like Transformer attention.

comments.title: Claimed degeneration to deformable convolution is underspecified
comments.quote: The proposed attention module will degenerate to deformable convolution (Dai et al., 2017), when L = 1, K = 1, and W′m is fixed as an identity matrix.
comments.explanation: The conceptual relationship to deformable convolution is plausible, but the exact reduction is not fully specified. W′m has shape Cv × C, with Cv often equal to C/M, so it cannot literally be an identity matrix unless additional assumptions are made, such as M = 1 or a block/channel-selection interpretation. Also, ordinary deformable convolution aggregates multiple kernel positions with learned convolution weights, whereas K = 1 gives one sampled point per head; an exact equivalence would need to explain how heads, attention weights, value projections, and output projections reproduce convolutional kernel positions and weights. The same issue recurs in the ablation discussion when a K = 1 setting is described as degenerating to deformable convolution without clearly satisfying the earlier stated conditions.

comments.title: FPN ablation conclusion is phrased more generally than the evidence supports
comments.quote: Because the cross-level feature exchange is already adopted, adding FPNs will not improve the performance.
comments.explanation: The ablation supports the qualitative point that adding a standard FPN gives no meaningful gain in the authors’ tested setting, and BiFPN changes AP only marginally. However, “will not improve the performance” is too general. Table 2 reports BiFPN at 43.9 AP versus 43.8 AP for the corresponding no-FPN row, which is likely within noise but is not literally no improvement. More importantly, a single ablation setting cannot establish that FPN-style fusion will not help across architectures, datasets, backbones, schedules, or hyperparameters. A more precise statement would be that FPN/BiFPN brings no meaningful or consistent improvement in their setup.

comments.title: Overbroad claim that DETR was the first fully end-to-end object detector
comments.quote: Recently, Carion et al. (2020) proposed DETR to eliminate the need for such hand-crafted components, and built the first fully end-to-end object detector, achieving very competitive performance.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended contrast: DETR removes several prominent hand-designed detection components such as anchors, heuristic many-to-one target assignment, and NMS, and uses a set-prediction loss instead. In that narrower sense, the statement captures an important contribution of DETR. What remains problematic is the unqualified phrase “the first fully end-to-end object detector.” Many earlier detectors were trained end-to-end in the usual deep-learning sense, even if they retained anchors, proposal mechanisms, or NMS; DETR itself also uses a hand-designed Hungarian matching cost during training. A more precise claim would be that DETR was among the first competitive object detectors to formulate detection as direct set prediction without anchors and NMS. As written, the claim is broader than what the context establishes.

comments.title: Slow convergence is attributed mainly to Transformer attention without sufficient isolation
comments.quote: The above-mentioned issues can be mainly attributed to the deficit of Transformer components in processing image feature maps. At initialization, the attention modules cast nearly uniform attention weights to all the pixels in the feature maps. Long training epoches is necessary for the attention weights to be learned to focus on sparse meaningful locations.
comments.explanation: The complexity part of the motivation is well founded: standard self-attention over image pixels has quadratic spatial cost, so high-resolution feature maps are indeed difficult to use directly. The claim about initialization is also directionally plausible, since randomly initialized scaled dot-product attention has symmetric expected weights over many keys. However, the passage goes further by saying that DETR’s slow convergence is mainly due to attention over image feature maps and that long training is necessary for attention maps to become sparse. That causal explanation is not fully established here. DETR’s convergence may also be affected by set-based Hungarian matching, randomly initialized object queries, absence of dense spatial priors, loss design, and optimization details. The later success of deformable attention supports the hypothesis, but it does not by itself isolate attention-map sparsification as the main cause. The statement would be safer if framed as a motivating hypothesis or as one contributing factor.

comments.title: Epoch-count comparison could be misread as wall-clock training speed
comments.quote: For example, on the COCO (Lin et al., 2014) benchmark, DETR needs 500 epochs to converge, which is around 10 to 20 times slower than Faster R-CNN (Ren et al., 2015).
comments.explanation: The intended comparison is clear from the surrounding text: DETR requires far more training epochs than common Faster R-CNN COCO schedules, and 500 epochs versus typical multi-dozen-epoch schedules gives the right order of magnitude. The concern is the wording “times slower,” which can imply wall-clock time or computational efficiency, whereas the evidence stated is only number of epochs. Epoch cost differs substantially between DETR and Faster R-CNN, and Faster R-CNN schedules vary across implementations. This does not undermine the qualitative point that DETR converges slowly, but the numerical comparison would be more precise as “requires roughly 10–20× more training epochs” with the Faster R-CNN schedule specified.

comments.title: Decoder cross-attention complexity omits the query-projection term
comments.quote: In it, _Nq_ = _N_ , _Nk_ = _H × W_ and the complexity of the cross-attention is of _O_ ( _HWC_[2] + _NHWC_ ).
comments.explanation: The surrounding discussion correctly starts from the standard multi-head attention complexity stated just above, _O_(_NqC_[2] + _NkC_[2] + _NqNkC_). Substituting _Nq_ = _N_ and _Nk_ = _H × W_ gives _O_(_NC_[2] + _HWC_[2] + _NHWC_), assuming the same accounting convention for query/key/value/output projections. The quoted expression drops the _NC_[2] term. I checked whether this might be intentional because _N_ is usually small in DETR and the authors are emphasizing scaling with feature-map size; under that interpretation, the omission does not affect the conclusion that decoder cross-attention scales linearly in _HW_. However, since the paragraph presents the expression as the complexity rather than only the spatially dependent part, it is technically incomplete and inconsistent with their own general formula.

comments.title: Uniform-attention initialization argument is stronger than the stated assumptions justify
comments.quote: Typically, with proper parameter initialization, _**U** m_ _**z** q_ and _**V** m_ _**x** k_ follow distribution with mean of 1 0 and variance of 1, which makes attention weights _Amqk ≈ Nk_[,][when] _[N][k]_[is][large.][It][will][lead] to ambiguous gradients for input features. Thus, long training schedules are required so that the attention weights can focus on specific keys.
comments.explanation: Interpreting the OCR in the most plausible way, this seems to mean that the projected query/key components have mean 0 and variance 1, and that the normalized attention weights are approximately _1/Nk_ at initialization. The intuition is reasonable and matches the paper's broader motivation: dense attention over many image pixels initially has little spatial selectivity. What remains problematic is that the statistical step is not quite implied by the stated assumptions. If scaled dot-product logits are approximately i.i.d. with variance near 1, the expected softmax weight is _1/Nk_ by symmetry, but individual softmax weights are not necessarily close to uniform; their concentration depends on the logit variance and on _Nk_. The subsequent causal claim that this directly leads to ambiguous gradients and hence long training schedules is also asserted rather than derived or empirically substantiated in this passage. This does not undermine the method, but it overstates an intuitive explanation as if it followed directly from initialization statistics.

comments.title: Full sampling does not make this module equivalent to standard Transformer attention
comments.quote: When the sampling points traverse all possible locations, the proposed attention module is equivalent to Transformer attention.
comments.explanation: I can see the intended analogy: deformable attention is being presented as a sparse/pre-filtered version of attention over image locations, so if the pre-filter admits every location, one might expect to recover dense attention. However, checking this against the preceding definition leaves a substantive mismatch. In the described deformable attention module, the sampling offsets and attention weights are produced by a linear projection of the query feature \(\mathbf z_q\); the weights are not computed from pairwise query-key compatibilities such as \(\mathbf z_q^\top U_m^\top V_m \mathbf x_k\), as in the Transformer attention definition reviewed earlier. Even if the sampled locations covered every pixel, the resulting weights would still be generated from the query side and location index, not from the key contents. Thus the module would become a dense learned/dynamic spatial aggregation, but not standard Transformer attention in the usual content-based query-key sense. The claim could be fixed by weakening it to say that the module would have the same dense support pattern as Transformer attention, rather than being equivalent to it.

comments.title: Claimed degeneration to deformable convolution is underspecified and dimensionally unclear
comments.quote: The proposed attention module will degenerate to deformable convolution (Dai et al., 2017), when _L_ = 1, _K_ = 1, and _**W** m[′][∈]_[R] _[C][v][×][C]_[is][fixed][as][an][identity][matrix.]
comments.explanation: The high-level intent is reasonable: deformable attention is inspired by deformable convolution, and with single-scale sampling and fixed value projections it should resemble a deformable-convolution-like sparse sampler. But the stated sufficient conditions do not quite line up with the definitions given just before. First, \(\mathbf W'_m \in \mathbb R^{C_v \times C}\) with \(C_v=C/M\) by default, so it cannot literally be an identity matrix unless additional assumptions are made, such as \(M=1\), or unless \(\mathbf W'_m\) is meant to be a block/channel-selection identity rather than a full identity. Second, ordinary deformable convolution normally aggregates multiple kernel positions with learned convolutional weights; setting \(K=1\) gives one sampled point per attention head, so the equivalence would also require explaining how heads correspond to convolution kernel positions and how \(\mathbf W_m\) and the attention weights reproduce the convolution weights. The conceptual relationship is plausible, but as written the reduction is not mathematically precise enough to be taken as an exact degeneration.

comments.title: Ambiguous mapping between feature-map indices and ResNet stage strides
comments.quote: In encoder, we extract multi-scale feature maps _{_ _**x**[l] }[L] l_ =1 _[−]_[1][(] _[L]_[ = 4][) from the output feature maps of stages] _[ C]_[3][ through] _C_ 5 in ResNet (He et al., 2016) (transformed by a 1 _×_ 1 convolution), where _Cl_ is of resolution 2 _[l]_ lower than the input image.
comments.explanation: The intended construction is mostly clear from context: the encoder uses four levels, with three taken from ResNet stages C3, C4, and C5, and a fourth C6 made by strided convolution. Under the standard ResNet convention, C3/C4/C5 have strides 8/16/32, i.e. resolutions lower by 2^3, 2^4, and 2^5. What gave me pause is that the passage simultaneously indexes the deformable-attention feature maps as x^l for l=1,...,L and refers to C_l as having resolution 2^l lower. If a reader carries the x^l index into the stride statement, they could incorrectly infer that x^1 has stride 2, x^2 stride 4, etc., whereas x^1 actually corresponds to C3. The surrounding reference to C3 through C5 largely resolves the authors' intent, so this is not a mathematical error in the method, but the index reuse is potentially misleading for implementation.

comments.title: FPN ablation claim is phrased more generally than the evidence likely supports
comments.quote: Experiments in Section 5.2 show that adding FPN will not improve the performance.
comments.explanation: The motivation is sensible: multi-scale deformable attention is meant to exchange information across feature levels, reducing the need for a hand-designed top-down FPN. I also recognize that this sentence points forward to an ablation, so the detailed evidence may appear later. The remaining concern is the wording 'will not improve the performance,' which reads as a general claim about adding FPN, whereas an ablation can only establish that FPN did not help under the authors' particular architecture, dataset, backbone, schedule, and hyperparameters. Since FPN-style fusion is known to be beneficial in many object detectors, the claim would be safer as 'does not improve performance in our setting' or 'in our ablation.' This is a framing issue rather than a flaw in the proposed method.

comments.title: Classification loss is described as applying to bounding-box classification
comments.quote: Other hyper-parameter setting and training strategy mainly follow DETR (Carion et al., 2020), except that Focal Loss (Lin et al., 2017b) with loss weight of 2 is used for bounding box classification, and the number of object queries is increased from 100 to 300.
comments.explanation: The likely intent is clear: Deformable DETR replaces DETR's class-label loss with Focal Loss for the classification branch associated with each predicted object/query, while box regression remains handled by the usual box losses. In detector terminology, one sometimes informally says that predicted boxes are classified, so the sentence is not necessarily wrong. However, 'bounding box classification' could be read as classifying the box coordinates themselves or as modifying the regression loss, which would be misleading for reproduction. A more precise phrasing would be 'Focal Loss is used for object/category classification' or 'for the classification branch of predicted boxes.'

comments.title: Degeneration to deformable convolution omits necessary conditions
comments.quote: When multi-scale attention is not applied, and _K_ = 1, our (multi-scale) deformable attention module degenerates to deformable convolution, delivering noticeable lower accuracy.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors’ intent: they are interpreting the lowest-capacity ablation as a deformable-convolution-like special case, consistent with the earlier discussion that deformable attention is inspired by deformable convolution. However, checking against the formal definition given earlier, the exact degeneration was stated with additional conditions: single-scale input, _K_ = 1, and _\mathbf{W}'_m_ fixed as an identity matrix. In the ablation table, the relevant rows appear to involve learned projection matrices and possibly multi-scale inputs without multi-scale attention, depending on which row the sentence is referring to. With learned value/output projections and multi-head structure, the operation may be similar to deformable convolution, but it is not strictly the same object unless those additional assumptions hold. This is localized and does not affect the empirical comparison, but the wording “degenerates to deformable convolution” is technically stronger than what the stated setup guarantees.

comments.title: FPN ablation conclusion is slightly stronger than the table supports
comments.quote: Because the cross-level feature exchange is already adopted, adding FPNs will not improve the performance.
comments.explanation: The intended conclusion is reasonable: the table shows that adding a standard FPN gives essentially the same AP as the no-FPN multi-scale deformable attention baseline, and BiFPN changes the result only marginally. Still, the wording “will not improve” is stronger than the evidence shown. In Table 2, BiFPN reports 43.9 AP versus 43.8 AP for the corresponding no-FPN row, while FPN reports 43.8 AP. A 0.1 AP difference is likely within noise and not practically meaningful, so the authors’ qualitative point is mostly supported. But strictly, the presented numbers do not show no improvement in all cases, and a single ablation setting does not justify the general future-tense claim. A more precise statement would be that adding FPN/BiFPN brings no meaningful or consistent improvement in their setup.

comments.title: “Without bells and whistles” is potentially misleading given the included variants
comments.quote: Iterative bounding box refinement and two-stage mechanism are both utilized by our models in Table 3. With ResNet-101 and ResNeXt-101 (Xie et al., 2017), our method achieves 48.7 AP and 49.0 AP without bells and whistles, respectively.
comments.explanation: The authors do explicitly disclose that the Table 3 models include iterative bounding box refinement and the two-stage mechanism, so the numerical comparison is not hidden. In object-detection papers, “without bells and whistles” often means without test-time augmentation, ensembling, extra training data, or specialized backbone modifications, and under that convention the phrase is understandable. However, a careful reader could also regard iterative refinement and a two-stage proposal mechanism as substantial architectural enhancements over the base Deformable DETR model, not merely the core method. Thus the phrase may make the comparison sound more bare-bones than it is. This is a framing issue rather than a numerical or methodological error, because the included components are stated immediately beforehand.

progressive__claude-opus-4.7.label: Progressive (claude-opus-4.7)
progressive__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

This paper presents Deformable DETR, a well-motivated and clearly-articulated extension of DETR that addresses two well-known limitations of the original architecture: slow convergence and poor small-object detection due to quadratic attention complexity over high-resolution feature maps. The core contribution—a deformable attention module that attends to a small, learned set of sampling locations rather than all spatial positions—is an elegant synthesis of deformable convolutions' sparse sampling with Transformers' relational modeling, and it naturally extends to multi-scale features without requiring an FPN. The introduction frames the problem crisply, the claimed 10× training speedup with improved small-object AP is a substantial and practically significant result, and the additional explorations (iterative bounding box refinement and a two-stage variant) broaden the paper's impact. The related work section is thorough, situating the method within the three main lines of efficient attention research. My primary concerns at this stage are: (1) the novelty relative to prior deformable convolution work should be more sharply distinguished beyond the "adds relation modeling" framing, as the sampling mechanism appears mechanically similar; (2) it would strengthen the paper to include more intuition or theoretical justification for why sparse sampling specifically resolves the *convergence* issue (as opposed to merely the complexity issue)—the current explanation that uniform initial attention requires long training to sparsify is plausible but not rigorously demonstrated; and (3) the evaluation is confined to COCO, so claims about general effectiveness would benefit from acknowledgment of this scope. Overall, however, the paper appears to make a clear, impactful, and well-presented contribution to end-to-end detection.

comments.title: Degeneration to deformable convolution claim requires additional unstated conditions
comments.quote: The proposed attention module will degenerate to deformable convolution (Dai et al., 2017), when _L_ = 1, _K_ = 1, and _**W** m[′][∈]_[R] _[C][v][×][C]_[is][fixed][as][an][identity][matrix.]
comments.explanation: I checked this claim carefully. Standard deformable convolution samples K=9 (3×3 kernel) locations per output, not K=1; setting K=1 would correspond to a 1×1 deformable conv, not the general deformable convolution of Dai et al. 2017. The authors likely mean 'K sampling points per head, with M heads acting like kernel positions,' but as written (K=1, L=1) this reduces to a single sampled value per head which doesn't match standard deformable conv unless M is interpreted as the kernel size. Also, W'_m being an identity requires C_v = C, which contradicts the default C_v = C/M stated in the summary. A careful reader could be confused about exactly what correspondence is claimed. This is a minor overclaim / ambiguity that doesn't affect the main contribution but the mapping to deformable convolution is not as clean as stated.

comments.title: Equivalence to Transformer attention when sampling points traverse all locations is imprecise
comments.quote: When the sampling points traverse all possible locations, the proposed attention module is equivalent to Transformer attention.
comments.explanation: Initially this seems like a reasonable limiting statement. On reflection, there's a subtle issue: in deformable attention, the attention weights A_mqk are computed by a linear projection of z_q followed by softmax — they are not computed as a query-key compatibility z_q^T U_m^T V_m x_k / sqrt(C_v) as in standard Transformer attention. Even if the sampling locations covered all HW positions, the weight computation mechanism is fundamentally different (content-agnostic of keys). So 'equivalent' is too strong — it would be equivalent in coverage/support but not in the functional form of attention weight computation. A careful reader could be misled into thinking deformable attention is strictly a generalization of standard attention, which it isn't. Minor overclaim.

comments.title: Category misclassification of deformable attention
comments.quote: Our proposed deformable attention module is inspired by deformable convolution, and belongs to the second category.
comments.explanation: Initially this seemed like a simple classification statement, but checking it against the taxonomy established earlier in the Related Work section raises concern. The second category was defined as 'data-dependent sparse attention' with examples like LSH (Kitaev et al.) and k-means (Roy et al.) — methods where the sparse pattern depends on the content of keys (hashing/clustering of key elements). In contrast, deformable attention predicts sampling offsets from the query feature alone (as stated right after: 'a small fixed set of sampling points predicted from the feature of query elements'), and the sampling pattern doesn't depend on hashing or clustering of keys. One could argue either way — it's 'data-dependent' in that offsets are query-dependent, but it doesn't match the defining examples (LSH, k-means, learned block permutations) that the authors themselves listed. A careful reader could reasonably find this claim imprecise. This is a framing concern rather than a technical error, since the authors clearly explain what deformable attention actually does.

comments.title: Complexity of DETR encoder self-attention omits C^2 term
comments.quote: The computational complexity of self-attention is of _O_ ( _H_[2] _W_[2] _C_ ), which grows quadratically with the spatial size.
comments.explanation: Earlier in the same section the authors give the general multi-head attention complexity as O(N_q C^2 + N_k C^2 + N_q N_k C), and note that in the image domain where N_q = N_k = HW ≫ C, the third term dominates giving O(N_q N_k C). So writing the DETR encoder complexity as O(H^2 W^2 C) is consistent with their assumption HW ≫ C (the projection terms HWC^2 are dropped as non-dominant). This is a standard simplification and the authors have already justified it, so while a reader unfamiliar with the earlier paragraph might wonder about the missing C^2 terms, the context resolves it. Not a real issue.

comments.title: Complexity of deformable attention: factor of 2 and query projection term
comments.quote: the complexity of the deformable attention module is of _O_ (2 _NqC_[2] + min( _HWC_[2] _, NqKC_[2] ))
comments.explanation: Initially I wondered why the factor of 2 appears explicitly inside big-O notation, where constants are normally dropped. This is stylistically unusual but not incorrect — authors sometimes keep small constants for readability. More substantively, I tried to verify the terms: the query undergoes a linear projection to produce 3MK channels for offsets and attention weights (cost O(N_q C·MK) = O(N_q C·MK), not O(N_q C^2)), plus there is a projection W_m on the aggregated values (O(N_q C^2)) and the W'_m projection on x (O(HW C^2) or O(N_q K C^2) depending on whether values are precomputed). The '2 N_q C^2' likely accounts for the output projection and the value projection W'_m when applied per-query, but the offset/weight prediction cost (O(N_q C MK)) is not obviously captured. The paper defers details to Appendix A.1, which is acceptable as a forward reference, but the '2' inside big-O and the absence of an MK-related term in the stated complexity could mislead a reader comparing to standard attention complexity. This is a minor framing issue resolvable by the appendix.

comments.title: Inconsistent indexing of multi-scale feature maps from ResNet stages
comments.quote: we extract multi-scale feature maps _{_ _**x**[l] }[L] l_ =1 _[−]_[1][(] _[L]_[ = 4][) from the output feature maps of stages] _[ C]_[3][ through] _C_ 5 in ResNet
comments.explanation: Initially I was confused by the OCR rendering, but reading carefully, the authors say they extract $\{x^l\}_{l=1}^{L-1}$ with $L=4$ from stages $C_3$ through $C_5$. That gives 3 feature maps (for $l=1,2,3$), and then $x^L=x^4$ is obtained from a stride-2 conv on $C_5$ (called $C_6$). So 4 levels total. This is consistent. However, there's a minor concern: the text says '$C_l$ is of resolution $2^l$ lower than the input image,' but with $l$ indexing 1..4 mapping to $C_3$..$C_6$, the actual resolutions are $2^3$..$2^6$ lower than the input, not $2^l$. The variable $l$ here appears to be overloaded — in '$C_l$' it seems to refer to the ResNet stage index (3..6), while elsewhere $l$ indexes the feature level (1..L). A reader could be misled, though the intent is clear from ResNet conventions.

comments.title: Encoder feature-level extraction range contradicts earlier description
comments.quote: we extract multi-scale feature maps _{_ _**x**[l] }[L] l_ =1 _[−]_[1][(] _[L]_[ = 4][) from the output feature maps of stages] _[ C]_[3][ through] _C_ 5 in ResNet
comments.explanation: The summary (from earlier sections) states L=4 levels are taken from C3–C5 (via 1×1 conv) plus C6 (from a 3×3 stride-2 conv on C5). Here, the OCR renders the index range as 'l=1 to L-1' with L=4, meaning three levels from C3–C5, then C6 as the fourth (x^L). The prose is consistent with the earlier description once the OCR is parsed correctly, but a reader could be briefly confused. This is resolvable from context and appears to be an OCR-rendering issue rather than a substantive error, so I won't flag as a real issue.

comments.title: Initialization assumption stated inconsistently (mean 1 vs mean 0)
comments.quote: Typically, with proper parameter initialization, _**U** m_ _**z** q_ and _**V** m_ _**x** k_ follow distribution with mean of 1 0 and variance of 1
comments.explanation: Reading this, I initially wondered whether the authors meant mean 1 or mean 0. The summary's Assumptions section records 'mean 0, variance 1,' which is the standard initialization assumption that yields near-uniform attention A_{mqk} ≈ 1/N_k (since softmax of zero-mean, unit-variance logits is approximately uniform). The '1 0' here is clearly an OCR artifact concatenating '10' or a line-break issue; the intended meaning ('mean 0') is the only one consistent with the subsequent claim. Not a substantive author error.
